draft.introduction: In Physics II.3, Aristotle presents his doctrine of the four causes—material, formal, efficient, and final—which together provide a complete explanation of why anything exists or changes. This framework structured Western scientific and philosophical thought for two millennia.

translation.text: In one sense, that out of which a thing comes to be and which persists is called a cause—for example, the bronze of the statue. In another sense, the form or the pattern is a cause. In a third sense, that from which the change or rest first begins is a cause. In a fourth sense, the end, that for the sake of which a thing is done, is a cause.
translation.original_text: ἕνα μὲν οὖν τρόπον αἴτιον λέγεται τὸ ἐξ οὗ γίγνεταί τι ἐνυπάρχοντος

translator_notes.note: The four causes (material, formal, efficient, final) are not four different things but four ways of answering the question “why?” Aristotle insists that a complete explanation requires all four. Modern science largely abandoned final causation, but the Neoplatonists considered it the highest.
